chunk_summary: Feeding management in infants under 6 months with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) focuses on restoring breastfeeding or safe alternatives, preventing complications, and ensuring effective feeding through techniques like supplementary suckling and therapeutic feeds, with close monitoring and follow-up.
learning_objectives: Understand the principles of feeding-first and dyad-focused management in SAM for infants under 6 months.
Learn techniques like supplementary suckling and relactation to support breastfeeding in malnourished infants.
Identify criteria for safe replacement feeding and discharge readiness in SAM management.
Recognize the importance of daily reassessment and follow-up in stabilizing malnourished infants.
key_facts: Management prioritizes breastfeeding restoration or safe alternatives, with skilled observation of attachment and milk transfer.
Supplementary suckling technique (SST) combines breastfeeding with supplemental milk via a thin tube to stimulate milk production.
Starter therapeutic feeds are temporary and should avoid overly concentrated formulas to prevent high solute loads.
Daily reassessment includes feeding effectiveness, urine output, temperature, weight trends, and maternal barriers.
Discharge criteria: effective feeding, clinical stability, and clear weight gain on a home-feeding plan.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Management of acute malnutrition in infants under 6 months is feeding-first and dyad-focused. The therapeutic goal is restoration of effective breastfeeding (or safe alternative feeding) while preventing hypoglycemia, hypothermia, and infection. (Source: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children, NBK154454)
document_key_topics: Safe replacement feeding and discharge criteria
Daily reassessment and follow-up in malnourished infants
